pi_items.source: All five domains are real, publicly registered websites with long histories. Google founded 1998, Amazon founded 1995, Wikipedia founded 2001, YouTube founded 2005, Twitter founded 2006.

Web search and Internet Archive documentation confirm google.com domain was registered September 15, 1997, and earliest Wayback capture is November 11, 1998. The metadata date range includes this date.

Web search confirms Amazon launched July 16, 1995 (before Wayback Machine archiving began), but Wayback Machine's first crawl of amazon.com was December 12, 1998. Multiple sources confirm no Wayback captures exist for 1995-1997.
Web search confirms wikipedia.org was first captured by Wayback Machine on July 27, 2001, which falls within the claimed date range.

Web search confirms YouTube domain was activated February 14, 2005, and earliest Wayback capture is April 28, 2005 (per Smithsonian Magazine and V&A Museum sources). This falls within the claimed range.

Web search confirms Twitter was founded March 2006, publicly launched July 15, 2006, and domain twitter.com was released September 2006. Earliest Wayback capture would be on or after September 2006, falling within the claimed 2006-2007 date range.
Task description and verifier.py both reference this as the required Firefox bookmark folder name. This is a task requirement, not a claim about external data.
Task setup script creates this directory path and verifier.py expects the report at this location. This is a task requirement.

privileged_info_summary: The task uses real Internet Archive Wayback Machine data. Four of five date ranges are verified as correct: google.com's earliest capture (November 11, 1998) falls within 1997-1999; wikipedia.org's earliest capture (July 27, 2001) falls within 2001-2002; youtube.com's earliest capture (April 28, 2005) falls within 2005-2006; twitter.com's earliest capture (September 2006 or later) falls within 2006-2007. However, amazon.com's metadata claims captures from 1995, but the actual earliest Wayback Machine capture is December 12, 1998 — a clear contradiction. Amazon.com was publicly launched July 16, 1995, but not captured by the Wayback Machine until late 1998. The task's date ranges will make it impossible for agents to find captures outside of the actual availability windows, but the amazon.com range beginning in 1995 is factually incorrect.
